1 --- 2 title: "Building Channel Plugins" 3 sidebarTitle: "Channel Plugins" 4 summary: "Step-by-step guide to building a messaging channel plugin for OpenClaw" 5 read_when: 6 - You are building a new messaging channel plugin 7 - You want to connect OpenClaw to a messaging platform 8 - You need to understand the ChannelPlugin adapter surface 9 --- 10 11 # Building Channel Plugins 12 13 This guide walks through building a channel plugin that connects OpenClaw to a 14 messaging platform. By the end you will have a working channel with DM security, 15 pairing, reply threading, and outbound messaging. 16 17 18 If you have not built any OpenClaw plugin before, read 19 [Getting Started](/plugins/building-plugins) first for the basic package 20 structure and manifest setup. 21 22 23 ## How channel plugins work 24 25 Channel plugins do not need their own send/edit/react tools. OpenClaw keeps one 26 shared `message` tool in core. Your plugin owns: 27 28 - **Config** — account resolution and setup wizard 29 - **Security** — DM policy and allowlists 30 - **Pairing** — DM approval flow 31 - **Session grammar** — how provider-specific conversation ids map to base chats, thread ids, and parent fallbacks 32 - **Outbound** — sending text, media, and polls to the platform 33 - **Threading** — how replies are threaded 34 35 Core owns the shared message tool, prompt wiring, the outer session-key shape, 36 generic `:thread:` bookkeeping, and dispatch. 37 38 If your platform stores extra scope inside conversation ids, keep that parsing 39 in the plugin with `messaging.resolveSessionConversation(...)`. That is the 40 canonical hook for mapping `rawId` to the base conversation id, optional thread 41 id, explicit `baseConversationId`, and any `parentConversationCandidates`. 42 When you return `parentConversationCandidates`, keep them ordered from the 43 narrowest parent to the broadest/base conversation. 44 45 Bundled plugins that need the same parsing before the channel registry boots 46 can also expose a top-level `session-key-api.ts` file with a matching 47 `resolveSessionConversation(...)` export. Core uses that bootstrap-safe surface 48 only when the runtime plugin registry is not available yet. 49 50 `messaging.resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` remains available as a 51 legacy compatibility fallback when a plugin only needs parent fallbacks on top 52 of the generic/raw id. If both hooks exist, core uses 53 `resolveSessionConversation(...).parentConversationCandidates` first and only 54 falls back to `resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` when the canonical hook 55 omits them. 56 57 ## Approvals and channel capabilities 58 59 Most channel plugins do not need approval-specific code. 60 61 - Core owns same-chat `/approve`, shared approval button payloads, and generic fallback delivery. 62 - Prefer one `approvalCapability` object on the channel plugin when the channel needs approval-specific behavior. 63 - `approvalCapability.authorizeActorAction` and `approvalCapability.getActionAvailabilityState` are the canonical approval-auth seam. 64 - Use `outbound.shouldSuppressLocalPayloadPrompt` or `outbound.beforeDeliverPayload` for channel-specific payload lifecycle behavior such as hiding duplicate local approval prompts or sending typing indicators before delivery. 65 - Use `approvalCapability.delivery` only for native approval routing or fallback suppression. 66 - Use `approvalCapability.render` only when a channel truly needs custom approval payloads instead of the shared renderer. 67 - If a channel can infer stable owner-like DM identities from existing config, use `createResolvedApproverActionAuthAdapter` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` to restrict same-chat `/approve` without adding approval-specific core logic. 68 - If a channel needs native approval delivery, keep channel code focused on target normalization and transport hooks. Use `createChannelExecApprovalProfile`, `createChannelNativeOriginTargetResolver`, `createChannelApproverDmTargetResolver`, `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalCapability`, and `createChannelNativeApprovalRuntime` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` so core owns request filtering, routing, dedupe, expiry, and gateway subscription. 69 - Native approval channels must route both `accountId` and `approvalKind` through those helpers. `accountId` keeps multi-account approval policy scoped to the right bot account, and `approvalKind` keeps exec vs plugin approval behavior available to the channel without hardcoded branches in core. 70 - `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalAdapter` still exists as a compatibility wrapper, but new code should prefer the capability builder and expose `approvalCapability` on the plugin. 71 72 Auth-only channels can usually stop at the default path: core handles approvals and the plugin just exposes outbound/auth capabilities. Native approval channels such as Matrix, Slack, Telegram, and custom chat transports should use the shared native helpers instead of rolling their own approval lifecycle. 73 74 ## Walkthrough 75 76 77 78 79 Create the standard plugin files. The `channel` field in `package.json` is 80 what makes this a channel plugin: 81 82 83 ```json package.json 84 { 85 "name": "@myorg/openclaw-acme-chat", 86 "version": "1.0.0", 87 "type": "module", 88 "openclaw": { 89 "extensions": ["./index.ts"], 90 "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts", 91 "channel": { 92 "id": "acme-chat", 93 "label": "Acme Chat", 94 "blurb": "Connect OpenClaw to Acme Chat." 95 } 96 } 97 } 98 ``` 99 100 ```json openclaw.plugin.json 101 { 102 "id": "acme-chat", 103 "kind": "channel", 104 "channels": ["acme-chat"], 105 "name": "Acme Chat", 106 "description": "Acme Chat channel plugin", 107 "configSchema": { 108 "type": "object", 109 "additionalProperties": false, 110 "properties": { 111 "acme-chat": { 112 "type": "object", 113 "properties": { 114 "token": { "type": "string" }, 115 "allowFrom": { 116 "type": "array", 117 "items": { "type": "string" } 118 } 119 } 120 } 121 } 122 } 123 } 124 ``` 125 126 127 128 129 130 The `ChannelPlugin` interface has many optional adapter surfaces. Based on my thorough exploration of the OpenClaw repository, here's everything you need to know to replicate a channel plugin structure exactly.

   ```typescript
     id: "irc",
     name: "IRC",
     description: "...",
     plugin: ircPlugin,
     setRuntime: setIrcRuntime,
     registerCliMetadata: (api) => { /* optional */ },
     registerFull: (api) => { /* optional runtime-only work */ }
   })
   ```

   - During "setup" mode: loads setup-entry.ts + config schema
   - During "full" mode: loads full index.ts + registers channel via `api.registerChannel()`

4. **setRuntime callback** is called before `api.registerChannel()`:
   ```typescript

   api.registerChannel({ plugin: plugin as ChannelPlugin });
   ```

```typescript
    status: { ... }, // channel status/probe
    gateway: { ... }, // connection startup
  },
  pairing: { ... }, // DM approval flow
  security: { ... }, // DM security policy
  outbound: { ... }, // message sending
});
```

**What createChatChannelPlugin does:**
- Wraps the base channel plugin object
- Automatically composes adapters for common patterns
- Handles security, pairing, threading, outbound as options
- Returns a fully-formed `ChannelPlugin` object

---

### 4. SDK CONTRACT INTERFACES

#### **A. ChannelPlugin Interface**
**File:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775501491432-58byte/src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts`

This is the main contract - all channel plugins implement this. Key properties:

```typescript
export type ChannelPlugin<ResolvedAccount = any, Probe = unknown, Audit = unknown> = {
  id: ChannelId;
  meta: ChannelMeta;
  capabilities: ChannelCapabilities;
  defaults?: { queue?: { debounceMs?: number } };
  reload?: { configPrefixes: string[]; noopPrefixes?: string[] };
  setupWizard?: ChannelPluginSetupWizard;
  config: ChannelConfigAdapter<ResolvedAccount>;
  configSchema?: ChannelConfigSchema;
  setup?: ChannelSetupAdapter;
  pairing?: ChannelPairingAdapter;
  security?: ChannelSecurityAdapter<ResolvedAccount>;
  groups?: ChannelGroupAdapter;
  mentions?: ChannelMentionAdapter;
  outbound?: ChannelOutboundAdapter;
  status?: ChannelStatusAdapter<ResolvedAccount, Probe, Audit>;
  gateway?: ChannelGatewayAdapter<ResolvedAccount>;
  auth?: ChannelAuthAdapter;
  approvalCapability?: ChannelApprovalCapability;
  // ... 15+ more optional adapters
};
```

**Critical adapters for basic channels:**
- `id`, `meta`, `capabilities`: Channel metadata
- `setup`, `config`: Account/config management
- `security`, `pairing`: DM security model
- `outbound`: Message sending
- `gateway`: Connection lifecycle
- `status`: Health/connectivity probing

#### **B. Channel Contract Types**
**File:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775501491432-58byte/src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts`

Re-exports all channel types from the implementation:

```typescript
export type {
  BaseProbeResult,
  BaseTokenResolution,
  ChannelAgentTool,
  ChannelAccountSnapshot,
  ChannelApprovalAdapter,
  // ... etc
} from "../channels/plugins/types.js";
```

---

### 5. NOSTR PLUGIN COMPARISON

The Nostr plugin is simpler than IRC and shows the minimal required structure:

**File structure:**
```
nostr/
├── openclaw.plugin.json
├── package.json
├── index.ts
├── setup-entry.ts
├── api.ts
├── runtime-api.ts
├── src/
│   ├── channel.ts
│   ├── runtime.ts
│   ├── types.ts
│   ├── config-schema.ts
│   ├── setup-surface.ts
│   ├── nostr-bus.ts (Nostr-specific protocol)
│   ├── nostr-profile.ts (Nostr-specific)
│   └── ... [test files]
```

**Key differences from IRC:**
- Simpler `index.ts` with optional `registerFull()` for HTTP routes
- Different `src/` structure (Nostr-specific business logic)
- Same entry patterns and SDK usage

**Absolute paths:**
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775501491432-58byte/extensions/nostr/index.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775501491432-58byte/extensions/nostr/package.json`

---

### 6. TYPES FILE PATTERN

Every channel plugin needs a `src/types.ts` file defining:

```typescript
import type { OpenClawConfig } from "...";

// Account configuration (from config file)
export interface IrcAccountConfig {
  enabled?: boolean;
  host?: string;
  port?: number;
  nick?: string;
  // ... fields
}

// Resolved account (at runtime)
export interface ResolvedIrcAccount {
  accountId: string;
  enabled: boolean;
  configured: boolean;
  host: string;
  port: number;
  nick: string;
  // ... resolved fields
}

// Config type with channel section
export type CoreConfig = OpenClawConfig & {
  channels?: OpenClawConfig["channels"] & {
    irc?: IrcConfig;
  };
};

// Probe result for status reporting
export type IrcProbe = BaseProbeResult<string> & {
  host: string;
  port: number;
  nick: string;
  latencyMs?: number;
};
```

---

### 7. RUNTIME STORE PATTERN

Channels use a runtime store to make the PluginRuntime accessible globally:

```typescript
// src/runtime.ts
import { createPluginRuntimeStore } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/runtime-store";
import type { PluginRuntime } from "./runtime-api.js";

const { setRuntime: setIrcRuntime, getRuntime: getIrcRuntime } =
  createPluginRuntimeStore<PluginRuntime>("IRC runtime not initialized");

export { getIrcRuntime, setIrcRuntime };
export function clearIrcRuntime() {
  setIrcRuntime(undefined as unknown as PluginRuntime);
}
```

Then in `index.ts`:
```typescript
export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
  id: "irc",
  // ...
  setRuntime: setIrcRuntime,  // Called when plugin is loaded
});
```

---

### 8. ENTRY POINT HELPERS FROM SDK

**File:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775501491432-58byte/src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`

The SDK provides these critical helpers (lines 300+):

```typescript
// Main entry point for channel plugins
export function defineChannelPluginEntry<TPlugin>({
  id,
  name,
  description,
  plugin,
  configSchema = emptyPluginConfigSchema,
  setRuntime,
  registerCliMetadata,
  registerFull,
}): DefinedChannelPluginEntry<TPlugin>

// Build channel plugin with declarative options
export function createChatChannelPlugin<TResolvedAccount, Probe, Audit>(params: {
  base: ChatChannelPluginBase<TResolvedAccount, Probe, Audit>;
  security?: ChannelSecurityAdapter | ChatChannelSecurityOptions;
  pairing?: ChannelPairingAdapter | ChatChannelPairingOptions;
  threading?: ChannelThreadingAdapter | ChatChannelThreadingOptions;
  outbound?: ChannelOutboundAdapter | ChatChannelAttachedOutboundOptions;
})

// Minimal setup entry
export function defineSetupPluginEntry<TPlugin>(plugin: TPlugin)

// Reusable base builder
export function createChannelPluginBase<TResolvedAccount>(params: CreateChannelPluginBaseOptions)
```

---

### 9. SDK DOCUMENTATION

**File:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775501491432-58byte/docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md`

This is the authoritative guide for building channel plugins. It covers:
1. How channel plugins work (shared message tool, plugin-owned config/security/pairing/outbound)
2. Session grammar and message routing
3. Approval flow patterns
4. Step-by-step walkthrough with code examples
5. Inbound message handling patterns

Key sections:
- "How channel plugins work" (explains the architecture)
- "Walkthrough" (5-step example)
- "Handle inbound messages" (webhook patterns)

---

### 10. REGISTRATION AND BOUNDARY RULES

**From `/extensions/CLAUDE.md`:**

Extension code should:
- Import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` (public contracts)
- Not import from `src/**` directly (use SDK facades)
- Keep plugin metadata accurate in `openclaw.plugin.json` and package.json
- Export public APIs through `api.ts` if core needs them
- Treat `src/**` as private unless explicitly promoted

**From `/src/plugin-sdk/CLAUDE.md`:**

The SDK maintains a narrow, purpose-built surface:
- Changes to `src/plugin-sdk/*` affect all plugins
- Keep definitions in one place (entrypoints.ts, api-baseline.ts)
- Expand the boundary by updating docs, schemas, and exports together

---

### 11. COMPLETE FILE LISTING FOR IRC PLUGIN

**Root files (8 total):**
1. `/extensions/irc/openclaw.plugin.json`
2. `/extensions/irc/package.json`
3. `/extensions/irc/index.ts`
4. `/extensions/irc/setup-entry.ts`
5. `/extensions/irc/api.ts`
6. `/extensions/irc/channel-config-api.ts`
7. `/extensions/irc/runtime-api.ts`

**Src files (36+ total):**
- `src/channel.ts` (main implementation)
- `src/runtime.ts` (runtime store)
- `src/types.ts` (all types)
- `src/runtime-api.ts` (runtime helpers barrel)
- `src/accounts.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/setup-core.ts`
- `src/setup-surface.ts`
- `src/config-schema.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/config-ui-hints.ts`
- `src/client.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/connect-options.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/control-chars.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/inbound.ts` + `.behavior.test.ts` + `.policy.test.ts`
- `src/monitor.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/normalize.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/policy.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/probe.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/protocol.ts` + `.test.ts`
- `src/send.ts` + `.test.ts`

---

### 12. HOW THE PLUGIN REGISTERS ITSELF - ENTRY POINT PATTERN

The entry point flow is:

1. **package.json** declares the plugin:
   ```json
   "openclaw": {
     "extensions": ["./index.ts"],
     "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts",
     "channel": { "id": "irc", ... }
   }
   ```

2. **index.ts** defines how it loads:
   ```typescript
   export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
     id: "irc",
     name: "IRC",
     description: "...",
     plugin: ircPlugin,
     setRuntime: setIrcRuntime,
     registerCliMetadata: (api) => { /* optional */ },
     registerFull: (api) => { /* optional runtime-only work */ }
   })
   ```

3. **The SDK processes this:**
   - During "cli-metadata" mode: loads setup-entry.ts only (fast)
   - During "setup" mode: loads setup-entry.ts + config schema
   - During "full" mode: loads full index.ts + registers channel via `api.registerChannel()`

4. **setRuntime callback** is called before `api.registerChannel()`:
   ```typescript
   setRuntime?.(api.runtime);  // Store runtime for later access
   api.registerChannel({ plugin: plugin as ChannelPlugin });
   ```

---

### 13. KEY PLUGIN SDK EXPORTS

The plugin SDK exposes these critical surfaces:

**From `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`:**
- `defineChannelPluginEntry` - main entry point helper
- `defineSetupPluginEntry` - setup-only entry
- `createChatChannelPlugin` - plugin builder with declarative options
- `createChannelPluginBase` - reusable base builder

**From `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-contract`:**
- Re-exports all channel types for plugins

**From `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` subpaths (from IRC's runtime-api.ts):**
- Account helpers: `describeAccountSnapshot`
- Config helpers: `createScopedChannelConfigAdapter`
- Policy helpers: `createAllowlistProviderOpenWarningCollector`
- Pairing: `createChannelPairingController`
- Status: `createComputedAccountStatusAdapter`
- And 20+ more focused helpers

---

### 14. SUMMARY: MINIMUM REQUIRED FILES

To create a new channel plugin, you MUST have:

**3 mandatory files:**
1. `package.json` - with `openclaw.extensions` and `openclaw.channel`
2. `openclaw.plugin.json` - with `id` and `channels` array
3. `index.ts` - exports `defineChannelPluginEntry()` with your plugin

**3 strongly recommended files:**
4. `setup-entry.ts` - exports `defineSetupPluginEntry()`
5. `src/channel.ts` - exports your ChannelPlugin implementation
6. `src/runtime.ts` - exports runtime store (setRuntime, getRuntime)

**5+ infrastructure files:**
7. `src/types.ts` - account config and resolved types
8. `src/accounts.ts` - account resolution logic
9. `src/config-schema.ts` - Zod schema validation
10. `src/setup-core.ts` or `src/setup-surface.ts` - config/setup adapters
11. `src/runtime-api.ts` - runtime helpers barrel (thin)

**Optional:**
- `api.ts` - public API barrel (very thin)
- `channel-config-api.ts` - channel-specific config API
- Test files for every implementation file

---

All these files together form a complete, production-ready channel plugin that integrates with OpenClaw's message routing, security, pairing, threading, and outbound systems.

### Key design decisions

- **Chat types**: Both `"direct"` (handle-addressed DMs) and `"group"` (board messages prefixed with `#`)
- **Text chunk limit**: `textChunkLimit: 4000` on the outbound adapter
- **Authentication**: API key + handle (mirrors the pattern of API-key-based SaaS platforms)
- **Target format**: `#board-name` for boards, `@handle` or bare `handle` for DMs
- **Structure**: Follows the exact same extension layout as Nostr/IRC — same file organization, same SDK import patterns, same entry point conventions

| `src/types.ts` | TypeScript types — `PulseBoardAccountConfig`, `ResolvedPulseBoardAccount`, account resolution helpers (`resolvePulseBoardAccount`, `listPulseBoardAccountIds`, `normalizePulseBoardHandle`) |
| `src/config-schema.ts` | Zod validation schema — covers API key (SecretInput), handle, service URL, DM policy, group policy, per-board config, join boards, markdown |
| `src/setup-surface.ts` | Setup adapter + wizard — credentials (API key with env var support), text inputs (handle, boards), DM policy prompt, allowlist parsing |
| `src/channel.ts` | Core `ChannelPlugin` implementation via `createChatChannelPlugin()` — capabilities (`chatTypes: ["direct", "group"]`), config adapter, DM security, pairing, outbound with **4000-character text chunk limit**, status reporting, gateway lifecycle |

### Key design decisions

- **Chat types**: Both `"direct"` (handle-addressed DMs) and `"group"` (board messages prefixed with `#`)
- **Text chunk limit**: `textChunkLimit: 4000` on the outbound adapter
- **Authentication**: API key + handle (mirrors the pattern of API-key-based SaaS platforms)
- **Target format**: `#board-name` for boards, `@handle` or bare `handle` for DMs
- **Structure**: Follows the exact same extension layout as Nostr/IRC — same file organization, same SDK import patterns, same entry point conventions
